1.blurb: When the first Jomon potters were firing their vessels around 14,500 BCE, Mesopotamia was a treeless wetland inhabited by Paleolithic foragers — the cultivation of wheat and barley in the Fertile Crescent would not begin for another five thousand years, and the first Sumerian cities would not rise for another nine thousand. The Jomon had pottery thousands of years before Mesopotamia did. The standard story that puts pottery downstream of agriculture runs in the wrong order on the Japanese islands.

Egypt would not produce pottery for another four to five thousand years after the first Jomon vessels and would not unify under a single pharaoh for ten thousand years. The Jomon were already deep into their pottery tradition when the first farming villages appeared along the Nile. The reader should keep this scale in mind throughout: prehistoric Japan begins long before any of the river-valley civilizations that usually anchor the start of world history.

2.blurb: While the Jomon were making flame-style pottery and managing chestnut groves at Sannai-Maruyama around 3500 BCE, Mesopotamia was producing simple, functional vessels — competent but plain, designed to hold beer and grain. Nobody else in the world was making anything remotely like the Jomon flame pots, and nobody anywhere else had the maritime trade networks that carried Itoigawa jade and Izu Islands obsidian across hundreds of miles of open ocean. The Jomon found a path to complexity that did not require farming.

Sannai-Maruyama was abandoned around 2300 BCE — within the same century that the Indus Valley civilization was building its great cities at Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa. The two cultures could not be more different: the Jomon, hunter-gatherers in pit houses with no agriculture and no writing; the Harappans, urbanites with paved streets, a mysterious script, and a region-spanning state. They lived on opposite ends of Asia in the same century and were unaware of each other's existence.

Egyptian pottery of the same period — the late Predynastic and early Dynastic eras around 3500–3000 BCE — was utilitarian and standardized, tied to the rapidly centralizing state and its administrative needs. The Jomon flame pots are the opposite: explosive ceremonial creativity by hunter-gatherer communities with no central state to standardize them, no granary economy to make them functional, and no reason to make them at all except artistic and spiritual.

3.blurb: The Polynesian voyagers were just beginning their great Pacific expansion (around 1500 BCE, the same period as the goggle-eyed shakoki-dogu) — sailing east from Taiwan and the Philippines on outrigger canoes that would eventually carry them to Hawaii and New Zealand. Two East Asian maritime traditions, on opposite ends of the same ocean basin, with no contact between them: the Jomon networking jade and obsidian across the Sea of Japan, and the Polynesians beginning the longest expansion in human prehistory.

The Shang Dynasty was emerging in China during the same period the Late Jomon was contracting — the late second millennium BCE. The contrast between contemporary East Asian societies could not be sharper: the Shang building palaces, casting elaborate ritual bronzes, writing oracle bones, and ruling through hereditary kings; the Jomon shrinking back into smaller communities, breaking dogu figurines in healing rituals, and arranging boulders into stone circles for seasonal gatherings. The continental and the island traditions were on completely different trajectories.

3.matchText: Mesopotamia and Egypt are building their first true monumental architecture
3.blurb: The Oyu and Komakino stone circles of the Late Jomon date to roughly the same period (2000 BCE) as the great monumental construction phases in Mesopotamia (the Third Dynasty of Ur and its ziggurats) and Egypt (the Middle Kingdom and the renewed pyramid-building of the Twelfth Dynasty). All three were investments of community labor in monumental ceremony — but only the Jomon version was being built by hunter-gatherer-fishers without a state, without a king, and without writing.

4.blurb: The Korean side of the Yayoi transformation: continental wet-rice agriculture, bronze metallurgy, and iron working spread south down the Korean peninsula through the late second and first millennia BCE before crossing the strait to Kyushu. The Yayoi immigrants were not coming from a vacuum — they were the southward edge of a much larger continental migration and technology spread that had been reshaping Korea for several centuries before it reached Japan.

The Yayoi transformation began around 900 BCE — the same period as the Zhou Dynasty in China, which had overthrown the Shang in 1046 BCE and was developing the political theory of the Mandate of Heaven. The cascade of cultural transmission from Zhou China through Korea to Japan that defines the next thousand years of East Asian history was just beginning. Japan was at the receiving end of a chain that ran continental China to peninsular Korea to island Japan.

The Yayoi transformation occupies the same world-historical centuries as the rise of the Maurya Empire under Chandragupta and Ashoka in India, the founding of the Qin and Han Dynasties in China, and the early Roman Republic in the Mediterranean. The post-Vedic period of north Indian state formation, with its iron tools and rice agriculture and emerging urban centers, was the South Asian parallel to the East Asian transformation that was reshaping the Japanese islands at the same time.

5.blurb: The Han Dynasty was the great Chinese state of this period, and Japan's first formal diplomatic contact with it (the Gold Seal of the King of Na granted by Emperor Guangwu in 57 CE) integrated the Japanese chiefdoms into the Han tributary system at the lowest rung. Han bronze mirrors became the prestige object of choice in late Yayoi elite burials, and the Han chronicles produced the first surviving written descriptions of Japan as a foreign land called Wa.

The early Three Kingdoms period in Korea — Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla emerging from the earlier confederations in the centuries around 100 BCE to 100 CE — was the political world that connected Yayoi Japan to the Chinese mainland. Korean polities were the bridge through which Han bronze mirrors, iron technology, and continental trade goods reached the Japanese chiefdoms. The Korean intermediaries are the reason Japanese prehistory has any documentary visibility at all in this period.

6.matchText: Three Kingdoms that divided China after the fall of the Han Dynasty
6.blurb: The Wei kingdom that hosted Himiko's envoys in 238 CE was one of the three Chinese states that succeeded the collapsed Han Dynasty in the early third century. The post-Han fragmentation of China into Wei, Shu, and Wu coincided exactly with the consolidation of Yamatai under Himiko in Japan — both regions undergoing political recombination at the same historical moment, with Wei diplomatic recognition becoming the prestige resource that Himiko's confederation needed to legitimize itself.

Korean shamanic political traditions of the same period show similar patterns to Himiko's Yamatai: female religious specialists with significant social and political authority, organized around trance, divination, and ancestor intercession. Himiko was not an isolated curiosity. She was the first surviving Japanese example of a political-religious role that was widespread across Northeast Asia, with strong parallels in contemporary Korean and Siberian shamanic cultures.

7.blurb: The Moche state on the coast of Peru was at its height during the same centuries as the Kofun period in Japan, building enormous adobe pyramids (the Huaca del Sol and the Huaca de la Luna) and producing some of the most spectacular ceramic art in the Americas. Two civilizations on opposite sides of the Pacific Ocean were producing comparable monumental architecture in the third through fifth centuries CE, with no contact between them and very different relationships to the written record. The Yamato chiefs built the Daisen Kofun without writing; the Moche built temple-pyramids without writing either.

The Korean side of the Kofun period: Baekje was Yamato's closest ally and most important source of continental technology, with Baekje princes raised at the Yamato court as honored hostages and Baekje envoys carrying specialized craftsmen and scholars across the strait. Korean influence at the Yamato court was so pervasive that perhaps a third of the aristocratic clans listed in later Japanese genealogies claimed Korean ancestry. The Yamato consolidation cannot be understood without the Korean half of the relationship.

7.matchText: Three Kingdoms and Western Jin periods in China
7.blurb: The Kofun period overlaps with the Six Dynasties era in China — the long fragmented post-Han centuries when the Chinese north and south were divided among rival states, with the Western Jin briefly reuniting the country before fragmenting again. The continued Chinese diplomatic presence in Korea and the regular technological transfer from Chinese sources through Korean intermediaries continued through this entire period, providing the upstream end of the East Asian cultural cascade that was reshaping Yamato Japan.

8.blurb: The Korean side of the 538 Buddha-statue gift: King Seong of Baekje was a devout Buddhist monarch whose kingdom had been one of the first Korean states to formally adopt Buddhism (in 384 CE under King Chimnyu). Sending the statue and sutras to Yamato in 538 was simultaneously a religious offering, a diplomatic alliance proposal, and an attempt to bring Yamato into the Buddhist East Asian sphere as a Baekje ally against Buddhist Silla and Buddhist Goguryeo.

Silla was Baekje's eastern Korean rival and would eventually unify the Korean peninsula in the late seventh century, defeating both Baekje and Goguryeo with Tang Chinese assistance. Silla's adoption of Buddhism (formally in 527 CE under King Beopheung) was roughly simultaneous with the Baekje gift to Japan, and the entire Korean peninsula was undergoing a Buddhist-political transformation that the 538 envoy was trying to draw the Yamato court into supporting on Baekje's side.

The Buddhist religious-political world that the 538 statue brought Japan into was the same world that defined the Northern and Southern Dynasties period in China. Chinese Buddhism, having absorbed Indian textual traditions through three centuries of translation work, was at this point the most sophisticated religious-philosophical system in East Asia, and Korean Buddhism (and now Japanese Buddhism) was downstream of the Chinese tradition. Japan in 538 was joining a Buddhist East Asian world whose intellectual center was somewhere between Luoyang and Jiankang.
